story:
Aaron’s lounge gleamed like a magazine spread—windows bright, cushions plumped, coffee table styled just so. After months of renovating through a fog of job-loss depression, his house was finally ready for sale; the photos were done, the place had never looked better. He cranked the stereo, air-guitared with his beer, and felt something he hadn’t felt in a while: light. He was proud of one more thing—he had finally told Larry no.

In the bathroom, midstream, the music cut out. Not a power outage—the lights still worked, the breakers were fine. He stepped back into the lounge and stopped dead. Larry stood there in full black motorcycle gear, visor down, a heavy, padded shape soaking up the light, turning Aaron’s perfect room into a backdrop for menace. “I turned off your music,” Larry said.

They circled the small things. Tea or coffee. 7-Eleven or the Vietnamese place. Larry wanted cheap. He wanted control. He took off his helmet but not the edge. “I came for my nails,” he called—brad nails from when he’d lent Aaron his nail gun for trim work.

Aaron stalled. He’d organized the storage room into a miracle of order and didn’t want to tear it apart two days before the open house. He offered cash instead. Larry stared. “I want my nails. I’ve got time to wait.” He tossed in a dig about an “opportunity” Aaron had blown—easy money Larry had given to an Airtasker for fifty bucks—and another about compliments going to people’s heads. Aaron kept it civil and poured tea with milk. Larry wanted it black but drank it anyway.

Aaron took a few swigs of beer in the kitchen, thinking, not again. He wasn’t dismantling that room for a box of nails. He pulled the first storage tub, then the next, trying to look without unraveling the whole system, while Larry announced calmly that Aaron wouldn’t be driving anywhere—he was already five beers down.

The doorbell rang. On the cam: two officers in checkered caps, a third circling Larry’s bike. “We’re looking for Larry Flowers,” one said. Inside, the officer’s voice carried: “We’re conducting a missing-person inquiry. Stavros Pappas hasn’t been seen. We have reason to believe you made threatening remarks toward him—captured on his home security. You’re not under arrest, but we need you to come to the station to assist with inquiries.”

Aaron told the second officer he knew Stavros—they’d all worked together until the restructure. Stavros was a chemist, like Larry. They used to have coffee. Then something soured; Stavros stopped taking Aaron’s calls months back.

Larry walked out with a smirk. “You can get back to your beer now,” he said, and slid into the back of the police car. The cars pulled away. The motorcycle stayed.

How did they know he was here? Back inside, Larry’s backpack sat by the couch. Aaron opened it, guilty but compelled. On top: a zip-lock bag of fine, pepper-like powder and a fresh box of brad nails. So Larry hadn’t needed the nails after all.

Aaron reached for whiskey, paused, and looked at his half-finished beer. Dark flecks dusted the rim. He poured the beer down the sink, wiped the immaculate stone, and poured a straight whiskey instead—cold, clean, and untainted. Boundaries re-drawn. The house held its breath, spotless and silent, as the sun fell in through the shutters.
